name: B.A. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan follows a well-ordered CS spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288/CS 331/CS 332 -> CS 350/CS 356 -> CS 435/CS 490 -> CS 491 Senior Project) with a matching math track (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 333), and prerequisite depths climb smoothly from 0 to 6 with no abrupt jumps. As a B.A. it leans on generic elective placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') that leave the upper-level path less prescribed than the core.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the repeated generic elective placeholders into named recommended tracks or example course lists so students see coherent upper-level pathways rather than open slots.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensibly shaped with a deepest chain of 6 (CS 100->113->114->241->288/... -> CS 435 -> CS 490 -> CS 491), which is reasonable for a 4-year CS degree and shows no over- or under-constraining in the core. The 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, a lab, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine CS prerequisite breaks, so structural health is good but not clean.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or explicit code mappings for YWCC 207, YWCC 307 and PHYS 111A, and resolve the GER 200/GER 300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term plan of study and per-course descriptions are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes / educational objectives (e.g., what a graduate can design, analyze, and communicate) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded (14-16 credits each). The mix transparently spans a CS core, a math/science block, GER requirements, and multiple free electives appropriate to a B.A.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. GER vs. free electives) so the balance implied by the term plan is stated explicitly for students.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and required courses are legible, but the plan contains parsing artifacts that hurt clarity: several GER items are split across rows with catalog URLs bleeding into titles (e.g., 'Science with Lab Elective (http://catalog...' followed by 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)') and a stray 'B.A.'-context line '228 B.S. in Computer Science' appears mid-plan, plus a null-code FYS seminar row.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean up the plan-of-study rendering so GER titles are single legible entries without embedded URLs and remove the page-number/cross-program artifact ('228 B.S. in Computer Science').

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required courses cover current, employable fundamentals: databases (CS 331), operating systems (CS 332), networks (CS 356), computer systems (CS 350), Linux/Python/C/MySQL tooling (CS 288), advanced algorithms (CS 435), software engineering (CS 490), and a team Senior Project (CS 491). Notable modern gaps in the required core are AI/ML, cybersecurity, and cloud/data-science, which the B.A. leaves to unspecified electives, and CS 288 still foregrounds PHP.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface or require modern electives in AI/ML, security, and cloud/data engineering (or define a track), and refresh CS 288's stack emphasis away from dated PHP framing.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course has a present, specific description with real technical substance (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm design/proof and complexity, CS 356 on layered architectures/routing, CS 435 on graphs/hash tables), and none are thin or placeholder. A few (MATH 111/112) are terse topic lists rather than full descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with sensible, explicit grade conditions and OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better'), and the core chain is internally consistent. Clarity is dinged by inconsistent phrasing ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher'), the verbose IS 350 GER clause, and prerequisites that point at dangling GER placeholders.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives on the catalog page, which are currently entirely absent.
Clean up plan-of-study parsing artifacts: GER titles broken across rows with embedded catalog URLs and the stray 'B.S. in Computer Science' page-number line that bleeds into this B.A. plan.
Resolve the 5 dangling course references (YWCC 207, YWCC 307, PHYS 111A, GER 200, GER 300) by adding or mapping proper catalog entries.
Strengthen currency of the required/elective set by surfacing or requiring modern areas (AI/ML, cybersecurity, cloud/data science) and refreshing CS 288's dated PHP emphasis.

Replace generic elective placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') with named recommended courses or defined tracks and standardize prerequisite grade-condition wording.
